IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem—Japanese PAS/zero-anaphora in pro-drop languages—and removes heavy reliance on syntactic parsers by introducing a Grid-RNN multi-sequence model that "automatically induces features sensitive to multi-predicate interactions from word sequence information". Empirically it yields state-of-the-art gains on the NAIST Text Corpus (single- and multi-sequence models outperform baselines; multi-sequence improves Zero arguments by ~3.0 F and NOM zero by ~2.5 F, with a striking ~30-point gain reported for DAT), showing practical significance. By demonstrating that multi-predicate neural modeling without syntactic features is effective, it is likely to influence subsequent work on PAS and zero anaphora and be adopted or extended for other pro-drop languages, giving it strong (but not wholly transformative) impact. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem—Japanese predicate-argument (PAS) analysis and zero/anaphora resolution in pro-drop languages—and removes dependence on parser-produced syntactic features by introducing Grid-RNN multi-sequence modeling of multi-predicate interactions (Sec.4). Empirically it outperforms the prior state-of-the-art (Ouchi et al., 2015) on the NAIST Text Corpus, notably improving zero-argument F by ~2–3 points and dramatically improving DAT identification (reported ~30 points) (Sec.6.2), showing practical benefit. The method (Grid-RNN + residual deep Bi-RNNs) both challenges the need for pipeline syntactic information and suggests a general neural approach that is likely to be adopted and extended for other pro-drop languages and SRL/PAS research, opening meaningful new directions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—Japanese PAS/zero-anaphora in pro-drop languages—and removes heavy reliance on syntactic parsers by introducing a Grid-RNN multi-sequence model that "automatically induces features sensitive to multi-predicate interactions from word sequence information". Empirically it yields state-of-the-art gains on the NAIST Text Corpus (single- and multi-sequence models outperform baselines; multi-sequence improves Zero arguments by ~3.0 F and NOM zero by ~2.5 F, with a striking ~30-point gain reported for DAT), showing practical significance. By demonstrating that multi-predicate neural modeling without syntactic features is effective, it is likely to influence subsequent work on PAS and zero anaphora and be adopted or extended for other pro-drop languages, giving it strong (but not wholly transformative) impact.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—Japanese predicate-argument (PAS) analysis and zero/anaphora resolution in pro-drop languages—and removes dependence on parser-produced syntactic features by introducing Grid-RNN multi-sequence modeling of multi-predicate interactions (Sec.4). Empirically it outperforms the prior state-of-the-art (Ouchi et al., 2015) on the NAIST Text Corpus, notably improving zero-argument F by ~2–3 points and dramatically improving DAT identification (reported ~30 points) (Sec.6.2), showing practical benefit. The method (Grid-RNN + residual deep Bi-RNNs) both challenges the need for pipeline syntactic information and suggests a general neural approach that is likely to be adopted and extended for other pro-drop languages and SRL/PAS research, opening meaningful new directions.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper addresses a core NLP problem (Japanese predicate-argument structure analysis) and proposes neural architectures (stacked Bi-RNNs and Grid-RNNs with residual connections) to model multi-predicate interactions, which is squarely within top NLP/ML venues. It uses community standards for methods and evaluation: experiments on the NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 with standard data splits, baselines including Ouchi et al. (2015), F-measure reporting and significance testing (bootstrap), and implementation/optimization details (GRU, Adam, Theano). The framing, related work (Zhou & Xu, Ouchi et al.), and claimed improvements on zero/Dep arguments indicate the paper is well positioned for the venue's audience and expectations. | B(5): This is squarely within top-tier NLP scope: it tackles Japanese predicate-argument structure (PAS) analysis and zero pronoun resolution using neural sequence models and Grid-RNNs, topics of clear interest to the SRL/PAS community. The paper uses standard methods and evaluation for the venue (stacked Bi-RNN/GRU, Grid-RNN, residual connections), evaluates on the standard NAIST Text Corpus with F-measure and baseline comparisons (e.g., Ouchi et al., 2015), and demonstrates state-of-the-art improvements, particularly for zero arguments. The related-work discussion and experimental setup are positioned correctly for an NLP/ML audience.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP problem (Japanese predicate-argument structure analysis) and proposes neural architectures (stacked Bi-RNNs and Grid-RNNs with residual connections) to model multi-predicate interactions, which is squarely within top NLP/ML venues. It uses community standards for methods and evaluation: experiments on the NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 with standard data splits, baselines including Ouchi et al. (2015), F-measure reporting and significance testing (bootstrap), and implementation/optimization details (GRU, Adam, Theano). The framing, related work (Zhou & Xu, Ouchi et al.), and claimed improvements on zero/Dep arguments indicate the paper is well positioned for the venue's audience and expectations.

reviewer_b.justification: This is squarely within top-tier NLP scope: it tackles Japanese predicate-argument structure (PAS) analysis and zero pronoun resolution using neural sequence models and Grid-RNNs, topics of clear interest to the SRL/PAS community. The paper uses standard methods and evaluation for the venue (stacked Bi-RNN/GRU, Grid-RNN, residual connections), evaluates on the standard NAIST Text Corpus with F-measure and baseline comparisons (e.g., Ouchi et al., 2015), and demonstrates state-of-the-art improvements, particularly for zero arguments. The related-work discussion and experimental setup are positioned correctly for an NLP/ML audience.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper has a generally logical organization (task description, single-/multi-sequence models, related work, experiments) and key components (equations for Bi‑RNN updates and softmax outputs, description of Grid‑RNN and residual connections) are presented. However, the provided text contains many duplicated and garbled passages (repeated paragraphs about pro‑drop, joint/pointwise approaches, and leftover TeX output), which disrupt readability and make parts hard to follow. Notation is mostly consistent and definitions (e.g. h^{(ℓ)}_t, y_t = softmax(...), case labels) are given, but several figures/tables are referenced (Fig.1/3/6, Tables 1–3) yet not present here, and some sentence flow suffers from proofreading issues. With cleanup of repetitions, removal of stray TeX lines, and ensuring figures/tables are clearly integrated, the presentation would be much improved. | B(3): The paper is broadly well-structured (task → models → experiments → results) and states the main idea (Grid‑RNNs to capture multi‑predicate interactions without syntactic input) in the abstract/conclusion, but the provided text contains many presentation problems that hinder readability. There are numerous duplicated paragraphs and sentences (e.g., repeated blocks about pro‑drop languages and related work), stray TeX/log lines (“TeX output 2017.02.07:2041”), and odd footnote/parenthetical placements that disrupt flow. Notation and equations are mostly defined and usable, and figures/tables are referenced (Fig.1/3/6, Tables 1–3) but are not present here; this plus formatting errors makes some sections harder to follow despite an otherwise logical organization.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper has a generally logical organization (task description, single-/multi-sequence models, related work, experiments) and key components (equations for Bi‑RNN updates and softmax outputs, description of Grid‑RNN and residual connections) are presented. However, the provided text contains many duplicated and garbled passages (repeated paragraphs about pro‑drop, joint/pointwise approaches, and leftover TeX output), which disrupt readability and make parts hard to follow. Notation is mostly consistent and definitions (e.g. h^{(ℓ)}_t, y_t = softmax(...), case labels) are given, but several figures/tables are referenced (Fig.1/3/6, Tables 1–3) yet not present here, and some sentence flow suffers from proofreading issues. With cleanup of repetitions, removal of stray TeX lines, and ensuring figures/tables are clearly integrated, the presentation would be much improved.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is broadly well-structured (task → models → experiments → results) and states the main idea (Grid‑RNNs to capture multi‑predicate interactions without syntactic input) in the abstract/conclusion, but the provided text contains many presentation problems that hinder readability. There are numerous duplicated paragraphs and sentences (e.g., repeated blocks about pro‑drop languages and related work), stray TeX/log lines (“TeX output 2017.02.07:2041”), and odd footnote/parenthetical placements that disrupt flow. Notation and equations are mostly defined and usable, and figures/tables are referenced (Fig.1/3/6, Tables 1–3) but are not present here; this plus formatting errors makes some sections harder to follow despite an otherwise logical organization.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper states clear claims (neural single- and multi-sequence/Grid-RNN models outperform prior baselines without syntactic features) and provides experimental evidence on the NAIST Text Corpus with standard train/dev/test splits and comparisons to reimplemented baselines (Sec. 6.1–6.2), plus bootstrap resampling for significance. The mathematical descriptions (stacked Bi‑GRU updates (Eq.3), softmax output, Grid layer and residual connections) are standard and correct for the proposed architectures. Minor weaknesses: exact numeric results, p‑values and some implementation/hyperparameter details are not fully reported in the excerpt, and deeper ablation analysis of the multi‑predicate Grid design is limited, but these do not undermine the main experimental conclusions. | B(4): The paper states clear empirical claims (neural single- and multi-sequence models outperforming prior baselines on NAIST Text Corpus 1.5) and reports experiments using standard train/dev/test splits, proper baselines (including an Ouchi et al. reimplementation), and bootstrap resampling for significance testing (Sec.6.1–6.2). The mathematical descriptions (stacked Bi‑RNN/GRU and Grid‑RNN with residual connections) are standard and appear correct. Minor concerns are limited transparency of numeric results/hyperparameters in the provided excerpt and the large DAT gains being potentially affected by corpus annotation differences (noted by the authors), but these do not undermine the main experimental claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims (neural single- and multi-sequence/Grid-RNN models outperform prior baselines without syntactic features) and provides experimental evidence on the NAIST Text Corpus with standard train/dev/test splits and comparisons to reimplemented baselines (Sec. 6.1–6.2), plus bootstrap resampling for significance. The mathematical descriptions (stacked Bi‑GRU updates (Eq.3), softmax output, Grid layer and residual connections) are standard and correct for the proposed architectures. Minor weaknesses: exact numeric results, p‑values and some implementation/hyperparameter details are not fully reported in the excerpt, and deeper ablation analysis of the multi‑predicate Grid design is limited, but these do not undermine the main experimental conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims (neural single- and multi-sequence models outperforming prior baselines on NAIST Text Corpus 1.5) and reports experiments using standard train/dev/test splits, proper baselines (including an Ouchi et al. reimplementation), and bootstrap resampling for significance testing (Sec.6.1–6.2). The mathematical descriptions (stacked Bi‑RNN/GRU and Grid‑RNN with residual connections) are standard and appear correct. Minor concerns are limited transparency of numeric results/hyperparameters in the provided excerpt and the large DAT gains being potentially affected by corpus annotation differences (noted by the authors), but these do not undermine the main experimental claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper combines established components (stacked Bi-RNNs/GRUs, residual connections, and Grid-RNNs) and applies them to Japanese dependency-based PAS, introducing a multi-sequence Grid-RNN to jointly model multi-predicate interactions (Sec. 4) and removing reliance on syntactic features. It clearly differentiates from prior work (e.g., Ouchi et al. 2015, Zhou & Xu 2015) by modeling multi-predicate interactions end-to-end from word sequences (Sec. 5.3). However, the core methods (Grid-RNN, Bi-RNN/GRU, residuals) are existing techniques, so the contribution is a non-trivial application/extension rather than a fundamentally new method. | B(4): The paper applies and adapts existing neural components (stacked Bi-GRUs, residual connections) to Japanese PAS but introduces a notable, non-trivial idea: extending single-sequence RNNs to a multi-sequence Grid-RNN to capture multi-predicate interactions jointly (Sec. 4). It also explicitly targets removing reliance on syntactic features (claims of outperforming prior joint models without syntax) and distinguishes itself from Zhou & Xu (2015) by addressing dependency-based PAS and multi-predicate joint prediction. Overall this is a clear, well-differentiated application/extension of known methods to a specific challenging task rather than a wholly new algorithmic breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines established components (stacked Bi-RNNs/GRUs, residual connections, and Grid-RNNs) and applies them to Japanese dependency-based PAS, introducing a multi-sequence Grid-RNN to jointly model multi-predicate interactions (Sec. 4) and removing reliance on syntactic features. It clearly differentiates from prior work (e.g., Ouchi et al. 2015, Zhou & Xu 2015) by modeling multi-predicate interactions end-to-end from word sequences (Sec. 5.3). However, the core methods (Grid-RNN, Bi-RNN/GRU, residuals) are existing techniques, so the contribution is a non-trivial application/extension rather than a fundamentally new method.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies and adapts existing neural components (stacked Bi-GRUs, residual connections) to Japanese PAS but introduces a notable, non-trivial idea: extending single-sequence RNNs to a multi-sequence Grid-RNN to capture multi-predicate interactions jointly (Sec. 4). It also explicitly targets removing reliance on syntactic features (claims of outperforming prior joint models without syntax) and distinguishes itself from Zhou & Xu (2015) by addressing dependency-based PAS and multi-predicate joint prediction. Overall this is a clear, well-differentiated application/extension of known methods to a specific challenging task rather than a wholly new algorithmic breakthrough.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents solid experiments on the standard NAIST Text Corpus with standard splits and strong baselines (Sec. 6.1/6.2) and compares single- vs multi-sequence models, studies network depth and residual-connection effects (Table summaries / Sec. 4.2, 6.2). However, ablation coverage is partial (no analysis of input-feature choices such as adding syntactic cues or alternative architectures, and limited component-level analysis of the Grid-RNN), failure analysis is minimal (they only note they focus on intra-sentential arguments but give no error breakdown), and key reproducibility details (model hyperparameters, sizes, training regimen and exact Grid-RNN specification) are not fully provided. Overall the empirical evaluation is adequate but missing some important analyses and reproducibility details. | B(4): The experiments are reasonably comprehensive: evaluation on the standard NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 with standard splits, comparisons to strong baselines (Imamura+09 and Ouchi+15), significance testing, per-case-role breakdown (Table 3), and analyses of network depth and residual connections (Table 2) as well as single-sequence vs multi-sequence models. Related work coverage is adequate (Japanese PAS and relevant neural SRL work). However, the paper lacks detailed reproduction-level hyperparameters/architecture specifics (embedding sizes, layer widths, training epochs, optimizer settings beyond ‘Adam’), and it contains little error analysis or discussion of failure cases beyond excluding inter-sentential (Inter-Zero) arguments. These omissions prevent a fully thorough evaluation although the empirical evidence is otherwise solid.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents solid experiments on the standard NAIST Text Corpus with standard splits and strong baselines (Sec. 6.1/6.2) and compares single- vs multi-sequence models, studies network depth and residual-connection effects (Table summaries / Sec. 4.2, 6.2). However, ablation coverage is partial (no analysis of input-feature choices such as adding syntactic cues or alternative architectures, and limited component-level analysis of the Grid-RNN), failure analysis is minimal (they only note they focus on intra-sentential arguments but give no error breakdown), and key reproducibility details (model hyperparameters, sizes, training regimen and exact Grid-RNN specification) are not fully provided. Overall the empirical evaluation is adequate but missing some important analyses and reproducibility details.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are reasonably comprehensive: evaluation on the standard NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 with standard splits, comparisons to strong baselines (Imamura+09 and Ouchi+15), significance testing, per-case-role breakdown (Table 3), and analyses of network depth and residual connections (Table 2) as well as single-sequence vs multi-sequence models. Related work coverage is adequate (Japanese PAS and relevant neural SRL work). However, the paper lacks detailed reproduction-level hyperparameters/architecture specifics (embedding sizes, layer widths, training epochs, optimizer settings beyond ‘Adam’), and it contains little error analysis or discussion of failure cases beyond excluding inter-sentential (Inter-Zero) arguments. These omissions prevent a fully thorough evaluation although the empirical evidence is otherwise solid.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (the state-of-the-art joint model Ouchi et al. 2015 and the pointwise Imamura et al. 2009 reimplementation) using the same NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 splits and F-measure, with significance testing reported. It provides fair experimental conditions (same data/splits, reimplemented baselines), per-case and ablation analyses (depth, residuals, single- vs multi-sequence) and explicitly acknowledges why some recent neural works (Shibata et al., Iida et al.) are not directly comparable due to dataset/setting differences. Citations and discussions of relevant competing approaches (joint vs pointwise, English SRL parallels) are present and differences are clearly analyzed. | B(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines, notably the state-of-the-art joint model of Ouchi et al. (2015) and the pointwise baseline (Imamura et al., 2009) on the same NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 with standard splits (Sec. 6.1). Comparisons are fair (same data, F-measure, bootstrap significance) and the authors explicitly note why some recent neural works could not be directly compared due to different/unpublished datasets (Sec. 5.3). The paper analyzes differences (single-sequence vs multi-sequence/Grid-RNN, residual connections, depth) and reports per-case-role results and error analyses (Secs. 3–6), and it cites and discusses the most relevant prior approaches (Ouchi et al., Shibata et al., Iida et al., Zhou & Xu, etc.).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (the state-of-the-art joint model Ouchi et al. 2015 and the pointwise Imamura et al. 2009 reimplementation) using the same NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 splits and F-measure, with significance testing reported. It provides fair experimental conditions (same data/splits, reimplemented baselines), per-case and ablation analyses (depth, residuals, single- vs multi-sequence) and explicitly acknowledges why some recent neural works (Shibata et al., Iida et al.) are not directly comparable due to dataset/setting differences. Citations and discussions of relevant competing approaches (joint vs pointwise, English SRL parallels) are present and differences are clearly analyzed.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines, notably the state-of-the-art joint model of Ouchi et al. (2015) and the pointwise baseline (Imamura et al., 2009) on the same NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 with standard splits (Sec. 6.1). Comparisons are fair (same data, F-measure, bootstrap significance) and the authors explicitly note why some recent neural works could not be directly compared due to different/unpublished datasets (Sec. 5.3). The paper analyzes differences (single-sequence vs multi-sequence/Grid-RNN, residual connections, depth) and reports per-case-role results and error analyses (Secs. 3–6), and it cites and discusses the most relevant prior approaches (Ouchi et al., Shibata et al., Iida et al., Zhou & Xu, etc.).
